Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂的制备及其在电解水析氧反应中的性能研究 卢 瑶 1    邹 华1*   张爱迪2   刘 勇2   黄守双3* （1. 国家动力电池创新中心燃料电池分中心 , 上海市燃料电池技术创新中心 , 上海重塑能源 科技有限公司 , 上海  201800；2. 江苏省功能高分子膜材料工程研究中心 , 江苏省高分子新 材料工程技术研究中心 , 南京贝迪新材料科技 (集团 )股份有限公司 , 南京  211103； 3. 上海 大学 , 环境与化学工程学院 , 上海   200444 ) 摘 要  电解水制氢是实现清洁能源转化的有效途径 ，其中析氧反应 (OER)作为电解水的阳极 反应，对整体能耗起关键作用。然而， OER的反应动力学较为缓慢，且通常需要贵金属催化剂 (如RuO 2或者 IrO 2)来降低过电位，这显著提高了制氢成本，限制了 电解水制氢技术的大规模 应用。因此，开发高效、稳定且低成本的非贵金属 OER催化剂成为研究重点。以普鲁士蓝类 似物为模板，通过高温退火法制备了 Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂。采用扫描电子显微镜、透射电子 显微镜、 X射线衍射仪、 X射线光电子能谱仪对复合催化剂的结构和形貌进行表征，通过电化 学工作站 测试了其催化性能。结果表明： Co3O4@Pt具有多孔纳米立方体结构，形成了 Pt和 Co3O4的异质结界面，提供了快速的电荷传输路径； Co3O4@Pt在低过电位下实现了高电流密 度，同时表现出较低的塔菲尔斜率和更高的双电层电容，表明其具有丰富的活性位点和优异的 OER动力学。基于以上优势，该催化剂有效提高了电荷转移效率，降低了 OER反应能垒，从 而提升了电解水效率。 关键词   Co3O4@Pt，电解水，析氧反应 ，异质结 中图分类号 TQ426                 文献标识码 A DOI：10.19817 /j.cnki.issn 1006 -3536.202 6.08.005 Preparation o f highly -efficient Co 3O4@Pt catalyst with enhanced electrocatalytic activity toward oxygen evolution reaction Lu Yao 1   Zou Hua1  Zhang Aidi 2  Liu Yong2  Huang Shoushuang3 （1. Fuel Cell Branch of  National Power Battery Innovation Center, Shanghai Fuel Cell Technology Innovation Center, Shanghai REFIRE Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai 201800 ； 2. Engineering Research Center of Functional Polymer Membrane Materials of Jiangsu Province, Engineering Res earch Center of Polymer Novel Materials of Jiangsu Province, Nanjing Bready Advanced Materials Technology (Group) Co., Ltd., Nanjing 211103 ； 3. Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444) Abstract  Hydrogen production through water splitting is an effective pat hway for clean energy conversion, in which the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), as the anode reaction of water electrolysis, plays a critical role in determining the overall energy consumption. However, the reaction kinetics of OER are relatively sluggish and it typically requires precious metal catalysts (such as RuO 2 or IrO 2) to reduce overpotentials, which significantly increases the cost of hydrogen production and limits its large -scale application. Therefore, it ’s urgent to develop efficient, stable, and low -cost non -precious metal OER catalyst. In this work, the hybrid catalyst of Co 3O4@Pt was rationally prepared using Prussian blue analogs ( Co3[Co(CN) 6]3) as templates. The structure and morphology of the composite catalyst were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X -ray diffractometer (XRD) and X -ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and its catalytic performance was tested by an electrochemical workstation. Results show that the resulting C o3O4@Pt exhibits a porous cubic structure, forming a heterojunction interface between Pt and Co 3O4, which provides a fast charge transfer pathway for OER. Based on these advantages, the catalyst greatly improved the charge transfer, reduced the reaction energy barrier, and thereby enhanced the catalytic activity toward water splitting. Key words  Co3O4@Pt, electrolysis of water, oxygen evolution reaction, heterojunction 收稿日期 ：2025 -07-23；修回日期 ：2026 -01-14 基金项目 ：国家自然科学基金区域创新发展联合基金项目 (U24A20286) ；江苏省科技成果转 化基金项目 (BA2023020 ) 作者简介 ：卢瑶 (1989 -), 女, 硕士 , 工程师 , 主要研究方向为电解水制 氢, E-mail：carol.lu@re - fire.com。 通讯作者 ：邹华 (1989 -）, 男, 高级工程师 , 主要从事氢燃料电池及其在新能源汽车 中的应用 研究，E-mail：hua.zou@refire.com 。 通讯作者 ：黄守双 (1985 -）, 男, 副研究员 , 从事无机功能纳米材料可控制备及其在电化学储 能及转化等领域研究 ，E-mail：sshuang@shu.edu.cn 。 近年来，全球气候恶化以及煤炭和石油等不可再生能源的日益枯竭，推动了对氢作为可 再生清洁能源的迫切需求，清洁氢也是我国未来能源产业的重要发展方向。电解水是一种有 效的制氢技术，但其作为氢气燃烧的逆反应，是一个吸热过程，分解水分子生成氢气需要额 外的能量。理论上，水的分解电压仅为 1.23V，但由于过电位的存在，实际反应能垒远高于 1.23V[1]。因此，降低过电位是提高电解水效率的关键。水分解过程包括阴极的析氢反应和阳 极的析氧反应 (OER)，其中 OER由于涉及四电子反应，其动力学缓慢，是控制水分解速度的 关键步骤，因此开 发高效、稳定且低成本的 OER催化剂至关重要[2]。过渡金属氧化物凭借 可变的价态、可调的电子结构、丰富的储量和良好的可扩展性，被认为是非贵金属 OER催 化剂的理想材料， 如 Fe2O3[3]、NiO[4]和CoO[5]等氧化物已在 OER中得到了广泛应用。 然而， 这些氧化物的催化性能往往受到导电性不足和反应活性有限的制约。 构建异质结结构能够在催化剂界面诱导电荷转移，从而有效改善电催化性能。 Zhang等 [6]的研究表明 Co/CoO异质结的界面促进了电子的快速转移，降低了 OER的过电位。 Gao等 [7]发现 Fe2O3/CuO复合材料促进了含氧中间体的吸附与活化，显著降低了反应能垒，提升了 OER活性。 Dai等[8]证实 CeO 2/CoS 1.97异质结的界面协同效应能够有效降低 OER过电位。上 述研究表明，异质结结构在不同氧化物间建立了有效的电荷传输路径，能够显著增强催化剂 的电化学活性和稳定性。除了材料的本征特性外，催化剂的形貌也对其电催化性能具有显著 影响。介孔催化剂因大比表面积、丰富的孔道结构和高效的物质传输能力在 OER中表现出 优异的性能[9]。然而，制备稳定、均匀的介孔结构通常较为困难，受限于复杂的合成工艺和 结构控制。为克服这一 难题，普鲁士蓝类似物 (PBAs )被广泛应用于制备介孔结构催化剂的模 板[10-11]。 这是因为 PBAs易于合成， 具有均匀的尺寸、 灵活的金属组成以及清晰的结构特征。 此外， PBAs具有良好的水热稳定性和可调的反应活性，使其成为构筑空心或多孔纳米结构 的理想模板，这种独特结构能够显著提高催化剂的活性位点暴露率，增强物质传质能力，从 而提升 OER活性和稳定性。 本研究以钴基普鲁士蓝类似物 (Co3[Co(CN) 6]3，简称 CoCo PBA )为前驱体，通过与氯铂 酸溶液混合并在氮气气氛下退火，制备了 Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂。通过系列表征分析了复 合催化剂的结构、形貌及表面元素组成，并研究了其电催化性能。 该研究为电解水 OER催 化剂的设计提供了新思路， 开发的 Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂 有望在未来的清洁能源转换中得到广 泛应用。

file_name: Co_(3)O_(4)@Pt复合催化剂的制备及其在电解水析氧反应中的性能研究_卢瑶.pdf
content:
[接上页] 通过系列表征分析了复 合催化剂的结构、形貌及表面元素组成，并研究了其电催化性能。 该研究为电解水 OER催 化剂的设计提供了新思路， 开发的 Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂 有望在未来的清洁能源转换中得到广 泛应用。

1 实验部分 1.1  原料与仪器 乙酸钴、二水合柠檬酸三钠、钴氰酸钾、乙醇、 氯铂酸溶液、 去离子水等。 扫描电子显微镜 (SEM，Apreo 2S 型)，美国 Thermo Fisher Scientific 公司；透射电子显 微镜（ TEM，JEOL2100 型），日本 JEOL公司； X射线粉末衍射仪（ XRD，D/max -2200/PC 型），日本 Rigaku公司； X射线光电子能谱仪 (XPS，ESCALAB™ Xi+型）美国 Thermo Fisher Scientific 公司；电化学工作站 (CHI 660E 型)，上海辰华仪器有限公司。 1.2  CoCo PBA 纳米立方体的制备 CoCo PBA 的制备方法参考文献 [12]，具体制备过程如下：将 4mmol乙酸钴和 4.5mmol 二水合柠檬酸三钠溶解在 100mL去离子水中， 得到溶液 A。将2mmol钴氰酸钾溶解在 100mL 去离子水中，得到溶液 B。将溶液 A与溶液 B混合并搅拌 30min，随后静置老化 24h。最 后，用去离子水和乙醇多次洗涤、离心、烘干沉淀，得到 CoCo PBA 纳米立方体。 1.3  Co 3O4@Pt复合催化剂的制备 称取 60mg CoCo PBA 分散在 60mL乙醇中， 超声处理 30min后置于磁力搅拌机上搅拌， 缓慢滴加 1mL质量浓度为 20mg/mL 的氯铂酸 /乙醇溶液，搅拌 20h后离心收集产物，干燥 后得到粉红色粉末状 CoCo PBA@Pt 。 将CoCo PBA@Pt 均匀铺在瓷舟中 ，在马弗炉中以 2℃/min的升温速率升至 450℃保温 2h，冷却至室温，得到 Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂。 1.4  电化学性能测试 采用电化学工作站测试电化学性能。测试中，使用碳棒作为对电极， Hg/HgO电极作为 参比电极，涂覆催化剂的泡沫镍作为工作电极。线性扫描伏安法 (LSV)、循环伏安法及计时 电位法测试均在氧气饱和的 1.0mol/L KOH 溶液中进行，且使用 90%的内阻补偿以减少溶液 电阻的影响。 电位通过公式 ERHE = E (Hg/HgO) + 0.0592 × pH + 0.098 转换至可逆氢电极 (RHE)作为参 比，其中 ERHE为可逆氢电极电位 ，单位  V；E (Hg/HgO) 为汞 /汞氧电极电 位，单位  V；pH 为溶液的 pH。通过测量双电层电容 (Cdl)计算得出电化学活性表面积。 电化学阻抗谱 (EIS)分 析采用 5mV的交流振幅 ，设定频率为0.01~10⁵ Hz。 2  结果与讨论 2.1  Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂的制备过程 普鲁士蓝类衍生物作为一种金属 -有机框架材料 ，具有规则的三维孔道结构和良好的化 学稳定性 ，能够提供均匀且丰富的活性位点 。首先通过共沉淀法制备出尺寸均匀的 CoCo PBA纳米立方体作为模板 ，并将这些粉末分散在乙醇中并超声处理 。乙醇不仅仅作为溶剂 ， 它可通过其分子间作用力有效促进 CoCo PBA 纳米立方体的均匀分散 ，减缓团聚现象 。经过 超声处理 ，进一步破坏了 CoCo PBA 纳米立方体的团聚 ，使其在溶液中分布更加均匀 。将氯 铂酸 /乙醇溶液逐步加入到上述分散液中 。氯铂酸 /乙醇溶液呈现浅黄色 ，随着搅拌时间的延 长，PtCl 6²⁻离子通过静电吸引和配位交换与 CoCo PBA 发生相互作用 ，逐渐被其吸附 。经过 20h搅拌后，离心处理得到的上清液变为无色透明 ，表明 PtCl 62–离子已完全被 CoCo PBA 吸 附。将CoCo PBA@Pt 置于马弗炉中进行退火处理 。退火过程中 ，PBAs中的 Co2+离子在高 温下与氧气反应 ，生成 Co3O4纳米颗粒 ，并且这些纳米颗粒通过自发堆积 ，形成具有多孔结 构的立方体 。此多孔结构为随后还原形成的 Pt纳米颗粒提供了高度分散的负载基质 ，能够 有效提高复合催化剂的催化活性 。同时，在高温退火中 ，PtCl 6²⁻中的 Pt⁴⁺离子被还原为 Pt⁰纳 米颗粒，这些 Pt纳米颗粒均匀分布在 Co3O4颗粒的表面 。与此同时 ，模板中的氰基在高温 下分解，生成气态产物 NO 2和CO 2并被逸出 。通过上述化学反应和结构演变 ，最终制备出 Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂 。

file_name: Co_(3)O_(4)@Pt复合催化剂的制备及其在电解水析氧反应中的性能研究_卢瑶.pdf
content:
[接上页] 与此同时 ，模板中的氰基在高温 下分解，生成气态产物 NO 2和CO 2并被逸出 。 通过上述化学反应和结构演变 ，最终制备出 Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂 。

2.2  结构表征分析 2.2.1  SEM 及TEM分析 CoCo PBA 、CoCo PBA@Pt 的SEM图及 TEM图见图 1。由图 1(a)可知：CoCo PBA 为 规则的立方体结构 ，颗粒均匀且表面光滑 ，平均粒径约为 1μm。TEM分析显示 CoCo PBA 为实心结构 [见图 1(b)]。这些形貌特征表明 CoCo PBA 作为模板在形态控制上具备良好的稳 定性和一致性 。由图 1(c)和图 1(d)可知，吸附 PtCl 62⁻离子后，CoCo PBA 的立方体形貌与粒 径并未发生显著变化 ，表面依然保持光滑且结构完整 ，表明 CoCo PBA 作为模板其形态和尺 寸在吸附过程中保持了较高的稳定性 。 图1  CoCo PBA 、CoCo PBA@Pt 的SEM图及 TEM图 [(a) CoCo PBA 的SEM图；(b) CoCo PBA 的TEM图；(c) CoCo PBA@Pt 的SEM图； (d) CoCo PBA@Pt 的TEM图] Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂的 微观形貌图及 选区电子衍射 (SAED )图见图 2。由图 2(a)可知， 复合催化剂在宏观上仍然保持了 CoCo PBA 模板的立方体形貌。然而，从高倍率 SEM图[见 图2(b)]中可以看到，这些立方体由纳米颗粒堆积而成。这种由纳米颗粒自组装形成的多孔 结构，源于退火过程 中有机配体的分解以及纳米颗粒的自组装和自发重构。多孔结构极大地 增加了催化剂的比表面积， 从而提升了活性位点的本征密度， 有助于显著增强催化性能[13-14]。 TEM表征进一步揭示了催化剂内部的微观结构，纳米立方体内部形成了明显的孔洞 [见图 2(c)]，有助于提高反应物的传质效率。为了深入分析退火过程中的结构演变，进行了 SAED 分析，结果如图 2(d)所示，其衍射环可分别归属于 Co3O4 (311)和Pt (111)。这表明在高温条 件下， CoCo PBA 中的钴离子经历了氧化过程，与氧气反应生成了 Co3O4。这种转变意 味着 CoCo PBA 在高温条件下被转化为具有更高催化活性的氧化物。

图2  Co 3O4@Pt催化剂的 微观形貌图及 SAED图 [(a)SEM 图，×50000；(b)SEM图，×200000；(c)TEM图； (d)SAED 图] 图3为Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂的高分辨透射电子显微镜 (HRTEM) 图及其局部放大图， 从 图中可以观察到清晰的晶格条纹，晶格间距分别为 0.28nm和0.26nm，分别归属于 Co3O4的 (220)晶面和 Pt的(111)晶面。 Co3O4和Pt在纳米尺度上相互接触并形成界面结构， 从而优化 电子的传递过程， 提高反应物的吸附和活化。 Pt纳米颗粒的均匀分布和高度晶化状态对于催 化反应至关重要，因为 Pt的(111)晶面通常被认为是最具活性的催化位点之一。这种稳定的 异质结结构有效增强了电荷转移能力，有望显著提高复合催化剂的催化效率和稳定性。

图5为Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂的 XRD谱图，在2θ=18.7 °、31.2°、36.9°、44.8°、55.7°、 59.4°和65.3°处观察到明显的衍射峰 。这些衍射峰分别对应 Co3O4标准卡（（JCPDS #42 -1467） 中的 (111)、(220)、(311)、(400)、(422)、(511)和(440)晶面，表明 CoCo PBA 在高温退火后转 化为 Co3O4物相。然而，XRD谱图中未观察到 Pt的特征衍射峰 ，原因是 Pt含量较低 ，导致 其信号强度不足以显现 。 图5  Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂的 XRD谱图 2.2.3  XPS 分析 图6为Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂表面元素的高分辨 XPS谱图。 图 6(a)为Co 2p峰的高分辨 XPS谱图，图中显示了 2对自旋轨道双峰和 1对卫星峰 (标记为 Sat.)。位于 779.05 eV 和 794.03 eV 处的自旋轨道双峰分别归属于 Co3+的2p3/2和2p1/2，表明催化剂表面被氧化，与 文献 [15]报道的结果一致。此外， 781.8eV和797.5eV处的自旋轨道双峰则分别对应于 Co2+ 的2p3/2和2p1/2，说明材料中 Co以多价态形式存在[16]。在 785.7eV和802.8eV处观察到的 卫星峰文献 [15]的研究报道相一致 ，进一步验证了 Co3O4物相的生成。图 6(b)为O 2p峰的高 分辨 XPS谱图， 与文献 [17]报道一致。 图 6(c)为Pt 4f峰的高分辨 XPS谱图， 图中位于 71.05eV 和74.26eV处的峰分别对应于 Pt 4f7/2和Pt 4f5/2，证实 Pt的存在，与 TEM分析结果一致 [18]。XPS表征结果说明， 复合催化剂样品中 Co3O4和少量 Pt的共存形成了稳定的复合结构。 图6  Co 3O4@Pt复合催化剂表面元素的高分辨 XPS谱图 [(a)Co 2p峰； (b)O 2p峰； (c)Pt 4f峰] 2.3   OER 性能研究 2.3.1  LSV 曲线及塔菲尔斜率分析 为评估制备的催化材料在电解水中的应用潜力，以 1mol/L KOH 作为电解质溶液，采用

content:
[接上页] 3. 1  LSV 曲线及塔菲尔斜率分析 为评估制备的催化材料在电解水中的应用潜力，以 1mol/L KOH 作为电解质溶液，采用

标准三电极体系测试了样品的 OER催化性能。样品的 OER催化性能见图 7。图 7(a)为样品 的LSV曲线图。 CoCo PBA 纳米立方体的 OER催化活性较低， 驱动 50mA/cm2和100mA/cm2 的电流密度所需的过电位分别约为 389mV和436mV。与此相比， Co3O4的OER性能有所提 高，电流密度 50mA/cm2和100mA/cm2条件下过电为分别为 344mV和400mV，说明热处理 提升了材料的导电性并产生更多的活性位点 。Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂表现出最佳的 OER性 能，电流密度 100mA/cm2时过电位 为340mV，与 RuO 2催化剂相当 (350mV)。其优异的性能 可以归因于 Co3O4和Pt之间的协同效应。图 7(b)为样品的塔菲尔斜率，根据塔菲尔斜率可 以推断材料的 OER动力学[19]。CoCo PBA 、Co3O4、Co3O4@Pt和RuO 2的塔菲尔斜率分别为 179.2mV/dec 、147.3mV/d ec、68.7mV/dec 和121mV/dec 。Co3O4@Pt的塔菲尔斜率明显低于 其他催化材料，说明其 OER动力学性能最佳，在电催化活性方面具有显著优势。图 7(c)为 样品的电压 -电流密度图。从图可以直观地观察到，在相同电流密度下 Co3O4@Pt所需的电 位最低，表明 Co3O4与Pt的复合结构显著提升了 OER动力学，赋予复合催化剂优异的电催 化活性。上述分析表明， Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂在电解水中具有广阔的应用潜力。 图7  样品的 OER催化性能 [(a) LSV曲线图； (b) 塔菲尔斜率； (c) 电压 -电流密度图 ] 2.3.2  电化学活性表面积分析 通过循环伏安法测试了样品的双电层电容 (Cdl)。Cdl是评估材料电化学活性表面积的重 要参数，能够反映催化剂表面暴露的活性位点数量[20]。在非法拉第电位区间内，通过不同扫 描速率下 阳极与阴极电流密度差值 （Δj）与扫描速率的线性拟合 ，其斜率即为 Cdl。样品的 电化学活性面积见图 8。 从图可以看到， Co3O4@Pt的Cdl值为 12.64mF/cm2， 明显高于 Co3O4 (8.77mF/cm2)和RuO 2 (4.42mF/cm2)。分析结果表明， Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂具有更大的电化 学表面积，暴露了更多的活性位点，从而在 OER过程中表现出更优异的催化性能。

图8  样品的电化学活性面积 2.3.3   EIS 谱图及稳定性分析 图9为样品的 EIS谱图。根据插图所示的等效电路模型，对 EIS谱图进行拟合可得到溶 液电阻 (Rs)、界面电荷转移电阻 (Rct)以及常相位元件 (CPE1 )，其中 Rs反映电解质和电极体系 的欧姆阻抗， Rct表征界面电荷转移过程的难易程度， CPE1用于描述电极界面的非理想电容 行为。对比不同样品可以发现， Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂表现出最小的界面电荷转移阻抗，表 明其界面电荷传输更为高效，从而赋予其更优异的电催化活性[21]。 图9   样品的 EIS谱图 除了催化活性，稳定性同样是评估材料 OER性能的重要指标。在电流密度 50mA/cm2 条件下，通过长时间计时电位法对 Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂进行稳定性测试。测试结果表明： 经过 12h的OER后，过电位微弱降低，这表明在长时间的反应过程中，材料表面生成了有 利于 OER的活性物种，从而进一步降低了过电位，提升了反应效率； Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂 不仅在 OER中表现出优异的催化活 性，还具备极高的稳定性，能够在碱性溶液中长时间保 持优异的性能。 3  结论

content:
[接上页] 测试结果表明： 经过 12h的OER后，过电位微弱降低，这表明在长时间的反应过程中，材料表面生成了有 利于 OER的活性物种，从而进一步降低了过电位，提升了反应效率； Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂 不仅在 OER中表现出优异的催化活 性，还具备极高的稳定性，能够在碱性溶液中长时间保 持优异的性能。 3  结论

以CoCo PBA 为前驱体 ，通过高温退火法合成了 Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂 。结构和形貌表 征显示，Co3O4@Pt复合催化剂具有多孔纳米立方体结构 ，并形成了高度分散的 Pt和Co3O4 纳米颗粒异质结界面 。Co3O4@Pt在OER中的催化活性明显优于单一的 CoCo PBA 和Co3O4 材料。在100mA/cm2和300mA/cm2电流密度下 ，该材料的过电位分别仅为 340mV和442mV， 可与 RuO 2贵金属基催化剂相媲美 。同时，其塔菲尔斜率低至 68.7mV/dec ，表明其 OER动 力学性能优越 。此外，较高的 Cdl值表明 Co3O4@Pt表面活性位点的充分暴露 。其性能提升 的原因在于 Pt和Co3O4的异质结结构 ，增强了电荷传输效率 ，同时多孔结构提供了更大的 比表面积 。 参考文献 [1] Zhu Y M, Klingenhof M, Gao C , et al. file_name: Huang 等 - 2017 - PtFe-NF electrode with high double-layer capacitance for efficient hydrogen evolution reaction in a.pdf
content: Pt/Fe-NF electrode with high double-layer capacitance for efﬁcient hydrogen evolution reaction in alkaline media Lirong Huang1, Yanping Hou1, Zebin Yu*, Zhenbo Peng, Li Wang, Jun Huang, Boge Zhang, Lun Qian, Leidan Wu, Zongchen Li School of Environment, Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Environmental Protection, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, PR China article info Article history: Received 15 November 2016Received in revised form8 January 2017Accepted 8 February 2017Available online 11 March 2017 Keywords: Pt particlesElectrocatalytic activityDouble-layer capacitanceHydrogen evolution reactionAlkaline mediaabstract The electrode with high catalytic activity, low hydrogen overpotential and low cost isdesired for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) via electrocatalytic water splitting. In this study, Pt/Fe-Ni foam (Pt/Fe-NF) electrode was synthesized via cathodic electrodeposition followed by impregnation deposition. Physical and electrochemical properties of Pt/Fe-NF,NF and Pt/NF electrodes were characterized by various techniques. The Pt/Fe-NFelectrode exhibited better electrochemical activity for HER under alkaline condition thanthose of Pt/NF and NF electrodes, owing to the introduction of zero valences Pt and Fe ontothe NF, and synergetic effect resulted from the formation of Fe-Ni alloy. Furthermore, Pt/Fe-NF electrode showed extremely high double-layer capacitance (69.1 mFcm /C02), suggest- ing high active sites for the Pt/Fe-NF. Tafel slope of Pt/Fe-NF was 59.9 mV dec/C01, indicating that the Volmer-Heyrovsky HER mechanism was the rate-limiting step. The Pt/Fe-NFelectrode with great electrocatalytic activity is a promising electro-catalyst for industrialhydrogen production from alkaline electrolyte. ©2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Introduction Hydrogen, as a renewable and clean fuel, represents one of the most promising new energy sources [1,2]. Water elec- trolysis is an important method for the production of highlypure hydrogen [3]. At present, the most commonly used catalyst for HER is platinum (Pt), since it has high catalyticactivity and very low over-potential for HER [4,5]. However, as a noble metal, its depletion and high cost hinders its practicalapplication. Hence, efﬁcient alternatives or reducing theusage of Pt are of great importance. It was found that theearth-abundant metals or their compounds, such as Ni, Co, Fe, Mo, Mo-P and Mo 2C, could be used as electro-catalysts [4,6e8]. Among these materials, Ni and its alloys, for example, Ni-Fe, Ni-Cu, Ni-Mo, Ni-Zn, and Ni-Co-Zn [9e12] have exhibited high chemical stability and excellent cata-lytic properties towards HER. It has been demonstrated thatthe effective catalytic activity of some transition-metals(such as Fe, Ni) for HER could be attributed to Brewer-Engelvalence bond theory [13]. That is, whenever metals of the left half of the transition series in the periodic table withempty or less-ﬁlled d-orbitals are alloyed with metals of the *Corresponding author . Fax: þ86 7713270672. right half of the series with more ﬁlled d-bands, a maximum in bond strength and stability of the intermetallic alloy pha- ses is expected, and there is a well-pronounced synergism inthe electrocatalysis, resulting in good electron-transportproperties as well as the efﬁcient absorption and desorptionof hydrogen in the process of hydrogen evolution [13].I n other words, the composite catalyst forms the synergeticeffect, which is advantageous to HER. Therefore, transition-metals are promising materials for HER. Despite of the HER catalysts, conductive supporting are also essential in electrochemical systems. The supportingmaterial is a crucial factor in determining the particle size and distribution of the metal particles located on its surface, which has a great inﬂuence on the amount of active sites,catalytic performance and stability of the supported catalyst[14,15] . The reported supporting materials include multi- walled carbon nanotubes [16], reduced graphene oxide [17], glassy carbon [18], stainless steels [19], and NF [20]and so on. Among these conductive substrates, NF is an ideal cathodesubstrate since it possesses good electrical conductivity, highspeciﬁc surface area, uniform pores, high H 2uptake capacity and good mechanical strength [21e25]. The electrodes are usually prepared by loading the cata- lysts onto the supporting materials, for instance, Pt/Ni [26] and Cu-Pt/NPCC [27]. However, preparation of the advanced Pt/Ni electrode not only required a large amount of Pt but alsofairly long reaction time [26]. Introduction of Cu into the catalyst could substantially reduce the amount of Pt;whereas, the standard potential of Cu (0.337 V vs. NHE) wasrelatively high, resulting in high hydrogen evolution over-potential and impeding HER on the electrode surface. Thus,the transition metal with lower standard potential would bemore satisﬁed as doping material. As mentioned above, Fe,which has been used as electro-catalyst material, has a low standard potential of /C00.440 V vs. NHE; furthermore, the less- ﬁlled 3d-orbitals of Fe (the electronic track arrangement of3d 64s2) would be paired with the 1s hydrogen atom to form Fe-H during HER. Meanwhile, as it is well known that theMeH bond energy (where M represents various metal ele- ments) exhibits a ‘volcano ’-shaped curve on heterogeneous catalysis [28]. Hydrogen adsorption energy that is too high would prevent the release of H 2at the surface of catalyst. In contrast, hydrogen adsorption energy that is too low wouldresult in slow electron-transfer rates. According to the ‘vol-cano ’-shaped curve, the Fe-H bond energy is moderate [28], which is favorable to HER. Hence, it is hypothesized that the combination of Pt, Fe and NF could be a promising cathode material for HER. Herein, in this report, we attempted to synthesize Pt/Fe-NF electrode using the electrodeposition technique and impreg-nation deposition method. The Pt/Fe-NF electrode was ex-pected to have the following superiorities: (a) catalytic activitycould be enhanced since both efﬁcient Pt with low over-potential and promising transition-metal Fe were loaded ontothe 3D porous, dendritic structured NF; (b) the addition of Fe isbeneﬁcial to the Pt loading on NF electrode; (c) the originalusage amount of Pt could be signiﬁcantly reduced as com- posite material was prepared, which decreased the overall cost of the electrode for HER. The performance of the as-prepared electrodes were determined in terms of onsetpotential, double-layer capacitance, charge-transfer resis- tance, Tafel slopes and stability, and so on. Experimental Reagents and materials Chloroplatinic acid (H 2PtCl 6), Na 2SO4and ethanol (C 2H5OH) were purchased from Guangzhou Jinhuada ChemicalReagent Co. Ltd. (Guangzhou, China); NaBH 4,H2SO4, KOH, and FeSO 4$7H2O were purchased from Shantou Xilong Chemical Factory (Shantou, China); Hexadecyl trimethyl ammoniumbromide (CTAB) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd; NF (poresper linear inch: 120 ppi, apparent density: 0.1 e0.8 g cm /C03, viod volume of the alloy: 60% e98%) were purchased from Suzhou Jialongde Foam Metals Co. Ltd. (Suzhou, China). All chemicalswere of analytical grade and directly used without furtherpuriﬁcation. The Ultra-pure water (18.25 M Ucm) was used for solution preparation. Preparation of Pt/Fe-NF electrode Pt/Fe-NF electrode was prepared by a two-step procedure,including: (a) cathodic electrodeposition preparation of Fedeposited NF (Fe/NF) electrode, (b) impregnation depositionpreparation of Pt/Fe-NF electrode. (a) Cathodic electrodeposition. Fe/NF electrode was syn- thesized using the electrodeposition technique [29]. This method could not only remove the use of polymer binders but also result in smoother surface and betternanoparticles dispersion on the electrode [29,30] . Brieﬂy, prior to the electrodeposition, NF electrode(5.0 cm /C23.0 cm /C20.17 cm) was ﬁrstly steeped with H 2SO4(1 M) for 5 min to the remove surface native ox- ides; then, NF electrode was washed respectively withC 2H5OH and Ultra-pure water for 15 min under ultra- sonic condition to remove the surface impurities. Afterthat, the pretreated NF electrode was used as the cath-ode and graphite (5.0 cm /C23.0 cm /C20.4 cm) electrode was served as the anode, and the electrodeposition was carried out for 30 min under a constant current of 10 mAcondition, and with a solution of 15 mM FeSO 4$7H2O, 10 mM Na 2SO4and 1 mM CTAB as the electrodeposition electrolyte. The addition of Na 2SO4was to improve the conductivity of the solution, while adding CTAB was toreduce the agglomeration of nanoparticles [31]. Then, the Fe/NF electrode was obtained. Fe was deposited onthe surface of NF electrode according to Eq (1). Fe 2þþ2e/C0/Fe (1) (b) Impregnation deposition. The as-prepared Fe/NF elec- trode was submerged in 50 mL of 0.6 mM aqueousH 2PtCl 6solution in a beaker and stirred with 120 r min/C01 for 4 h at 30/C14C. After soaking for 4 h in the Pt salt so- lution, the electrode was then soaking in 10 mL of 0.918 M NaBH 4solution (initial pH was adjusted to 12international journal of hydrogen energy 42 (2017) 9458 e9466 9459

Pt/NF electrode was prepared by impregnationdeposition method, the same as the second step of Pt/Fe-NFelectrode synthesis. Physicochemical characterizations The morphologies of the as-prepared electrodes were char-acterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-3400N), and the respective compositions were investigated byenergy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The phasestructure was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using adiffractometer (Rigaku D/MAX 2500 V, Japan) with Cu K a (l¼1.54056) radiation ﬂux at a scanning rate of 5/C14min/C01in the 2qrange of 20 e90/C14. And the accelerating voltage and applied current were 40 kV and 200 mA, respectively. Inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES, CAMT 2000 Thrmo Fisher Scientiﬁc) was utilized forquantitative determination of Pt loading amount on the elec-trodes. Liquid samples for ICP-AES were prepared through thefollowing steps: (a) Pt/NF (0.5030 g) and Pt/Fe-NF (0.5850 g)electrodes were weighed using an analytical balance; (b) eachelectrode was digested in 15.0 mL of aqua regia (mixture of HCland HNO 3with 3:1 proportion by volume) and heated at 120/C14C; (c) after the samples were completely digested, they werecontinuous heated to evaporate the aqua regia, then naturally cooled down to room temperature; (d) the samples were diluted with 5 mL of Ultra-pure water for ICP analysis. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) was carried out by Thermo ESCALAB 250XI with a monochromatic Al K aX-ray source. Pt/Fe-NF sample was ﬁrstly dried in the oven undervacuum, and then deposited on silicon wafer supports fortesting. And the obtained data were reported with the bindingenergy calibrated by C1s as reference energy (C1s ¼284.6 eV). Electrochemical measurements Electrochemical tests, including linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedancespectroscopy (EIS) and the Tafel polarization curves werecarried out with an electrochemical workstation (PARSTAT4000, Princeton Applied Research, USA) at room temperature.Particularly, all electrochemical measurements wereconducted in a typical three-electrode cell using NF, Pt/NF orPt/Fe-NF as the working electrode, a platinum gauze(1 cm /C21 cm) electrode as the counter electrode, and HgjHgO jOH /C0(1 M KOH electrolyte) electrode as the reference electrode with 0.05 M KOH solutions, which was purged withN 2for half an hour before the tests. According to previouslyreported literature, hydrogen production by alkaline water electrolysis is the simplest and environmental friendly method [32,33] , as compared with acidic water electrolysis since electrolyzer and electrode materials would suffer fromcorrosion under acidic conditions. LSV measurement was performed with a scan rate of 20 mV s /C01in the range of 0.3 to /C00.8 V vs. Hg jHgO jOH/C0, and the obtained data were reported with iR compensation. CV curveswere recorded in the potential range from 0.3 V vs.HgjHgO jOH /C0to/C00.4 V with various scan rates from 10 to 50 mV s/C01for different electrodes (iR compensation). The durability of the Pt/Fe-NF electrode was assessed by cycling the electrode for 1000 times with each cycle started at 0.10 V and ended at /C00.4 V with a scan rate of 50 mV s/C01(iR compensation). EIS were carried out in the frequency range of100 kHz e0.1 Hz. The obtained experimental EIS data were ﬁtted with an equivalent circuit (2R-CPE circuit model usingZsimpwin). The Nyquist plots were recorded from EIS results.Tafel polarization curves were collected in the potential rangefrom 0.2 V vs. Hg jHgO jOH /C0to/C00.8 V for different electrodes. The double-layer capacitance (C dl) was measured according to the method reported by previous work [34]. Results and discussion Physicochemical properties of the electrodes Fig. 1 a shows the SEM image of bare NF, which exhibits highly porous reticular structure. This structure is beneﬁcial formass-transport, owing to its large real surface area [21].I n contrast, surfaces of the Fe/NF and Pt/NF are rough and grainy,leading to relatively large exposed surfaces ( Fig. 1 b and c). The uniform and dense coating of Fe particles with diameters of around 300 nm on the surface of NF is obvious in Fig. 1 b.Fig. 1 c presents that the impregnation deposition Pt particles weredispersed on the surface of porous nickel electrode ratherthan inside the pores; these particles either grew indepen-dently or accreted together, with diameters of approximately200e500 nm. As can be seen from Fig. 1 d, the Pt/Fe-NF electrode surface was covered with a more compact, uneven coating. From theinset of Fig. 1 d, which shows the magniﬁed morphology of Fe and Pt particles, cluster-like morphology could be observed. Owing to the small particles deposited on the surface of NF, the effective surface area of Pt/Fe-NF should be increased ascompared with the NF electrode. The increase of surface areacould be favorable to catalytic activity and beneﬁcial to HER.To characterize the chemical composition of Pt/Fe-NF, EDXwas recorded ( Fig. 1 e). As listed in the inset table, the amounts of Fe and Pt on the Pt/Fe-NF electrode were 22.05 wt% and2.05 wt%, respectively, conﬁrming the successfully loading ofFe and Pt on NF through the present process. Pt loading on the electrode is an important parameter since it directly affects the performance of the electrode for HER in electrochemical system. Table S1 presented the theoretical Pt loading and experimental Pt loading on both Pt/NF and Pt/Fe-NF electrodes. And this could bepart of the reasons that Pt/Fe-NF exhibited better electro- catalytic performance than Pt/NF. Fig. 2 a shows the XRD patterns of NF, Pt/NF and Pt/Fe-NF in the diffraction angle range of 20 e90 /C14. Three broad peaks appeared at 44.496/C14, 51.849/C14and 76.381/C14were associated with the (111), (200) and (220) planes of Ni, respectively (JCPDSNo. 87-0712). Nevertheless, after impregnation deposition, thePt/NF and Pt/Fe-NF showed no Pt diffraction peaks, and thiswas likely due to the small loading amount of Pt (as conﬁrmedby EDS and ICP-AES results). Though the content of Fe on thePt/Fe-NF electrode was relatively high (22.05 wt % according toEDS data), no obvious Fe diffraction peaks were observed. And according to the inset of Fig. 2 a, the corresponding peaks shifted slightly to higher angles as compared to those of Pt/NF,indicating a contraction of the lattice upon substitution of Niwith Fe [35]since the radius of the Fe atom (124 pm) is smaller than that of the Ni atom (125 pm). Therefore, a possibleexplanation for no diffraction peaks of Fe being observed maybe ascribed to the formation of the alloy of Fe and Ni. In comparison with the Pt/NF and Pt/Fe-NF, it is worth notingthat the all intensity of Pt/Fe-NF diffraction peak becameweaker, which revealed that the addition of Fe possiblychanged the crystal form of Ni. In this case, the crystallinitydecrease may result from the transformation of the crystal size to the amorphous form. Compared with crystalline ma- terials, amorphous alloys can effectively reduce the activationenergy of the adsorption of hydrogen atoms on the surface,thus increase the catalytic activity for HER [36e39]. XPS measurement was employed to study the signals from the elemental compositions of Pt/Fe-NF particles surface. Ascan be seen from Fig. 2 b, the elements of Ni, O, Fe and Pt could be clearly identiﬁed on Pt/Fe-NF electrode. Fig. 2 c gives the XPS proﬁles of Ni element in the Pt/Fe-NF. Electrochemical properties To reveal the effects of Pt (0) and Fe (0) species on electro- catalytic activity, electrochemical measurements were carriedout. The LSV result in Fig. 3 a shows that the Pt/Fe-NF has the smallest onset overpotential ( h¼/C0100 mV) among three electrodes, implying that the catalytic activity of Pt/Fe-NF was the highest. What 's more, current densities of the electrodes arranged in an order of Pt/Fe-NF >Pt/NF >NF, indicating that HER can initiate on the surface of Pt/Fe-NF electrodes moreeasily than Pt/NF and NF electrodes. The estimation of effective active surface areas of NF, Pt/NF and Pt/Fe-NF electrodes was determined by the C dlin CV curves [41].Fig. 3 b and Fig. S shows CV curves of Pt/Fe-NF, Pt/ NF and NF at various scan rates (10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 mV s/C01) over the range of 0.3 to /C00.4 V vs Hg jHgO jOH/C0. The C dlof Pt/Fe- NF can be determined by plotting the Dj( ja/C0jb)a t /C00.1 V vs HgjHgO jOH/C0against the scan rate from the CV curve ( Fig. 3 c), which is expected to be linearly proportional to the effective surface area. The calculated values of C dlwere 69.1, 27.6, and2.30 mFcm/C02for Pt/Fe-NF, Pt/NF and NF electrodes, respec- tively. Obviously, the Pt/Fe-NF exhibited a much higher C dl than the other electrodes, indicating the high exposure of effective active sites. This could be attributed to uniform dis-tribution of the Pt and Fe particles on the electrode surface andthe special 3D porous, dendritic structured NF. As a conse-quence, Pt/Fe-NF possessed rich effective active sites and highoverall conductivity, leading to the superior HER activity [42]. The durability of Pt/Fe-NF electrode was also examined. Theas-prepared Pt/Fe-NF electrode showed negligible loss evenafter continuous 1000 cycles ( Fig. 3 d), indicating the superior long-time stability of the Pt/Fe-NF electrode in alkaline media. All the above results indicated that the Pt/Fe-NF electrode has excellent catalytic activity and stability. In order to highlight the advantages of the as-developed electrodes for HER and reveal the HER mechanism, Tafel andEIS tests of as-developed electrodes were performed, and theresults were shown in Fig. 4 . Tafel slope is deemed to be an essential parameter to reveal the inherent property of thecatalyst and recognized as the standard for the evaluation ofHER [43]. Meanwhile, Tafel plots have been widely used for quantitative kinetics analysis of the HER [44]. Based on the Tafel slopes and involved possible mechanisms, obviously, the value of Tafelslope for Pt/Fe-NF electrode was close to 40 mV dec /C01, sug- gesting the Volmer-Heyrovsky HER mechanism was involvedduring the HER process [44,46] and the electrochemical desorption step was the rate-limiting step [44,47] .S u c h small Tafel slope for Pt/Fe-NF electrode could be rationalized asfollows: (a) enhanced activity of Pt toward HER due toelectronic interactions between Pt and Fe; (b) high catalyticactivity resulting from the synergistic effect of Pt, Fe and Ni; (c)improved diffusion of reactants and products on the Pt and Fe surface resulting in more efﬁcient catalytic turnover [48]. Moreover, it has been demonstrated that a smaller Tafelslope means a faster increase of HER rate with the increase ofapplied potential [49]. The exchange current density (j 0) from Tafel test was used as a parameter to evaluate the activity of thecatalysts towards the HER [50]. The calculated j 0values were 1.494 /C210/C03mA cm/C02,1 . 6 9 2 /C210/C03mA cm/C02and 8.538 /C210/C03 mA cm/C02for the NF, Pt/NF and Pt/Fe-NF electrodes, respectively (Table 1 ). This suggested that the Pt/Fe-NF electrode was more active toward the HER. Therefore, the Pt/Fe-NF electrode couldshow an outstanding electrocatalytic performance for HER. EIS is an effective technique to investigate the interface reactions and electrode kinetics of the catalysts in HER [51]. Fig. 4 b exhibits the representative Nyquist plots of the EIS of the Pt/NF and Pt/Fe-NF in 0.05 M KOH solution. More detailedanalyses were carried out by ﬁtting the impedance data to anequivalent circuit (as depicted in the inset of Fig. 4 b). The Nyquist plots and ﬁtting results ( Table 2 ) revealed that the charge-transfer resistance (R ct) of Pt/Fe-NF (7.917 U) was much lower than that of Pt/NF (30.31 U), demonstrating the lower electron transfer resistance or faster electron transfer for Pt/Fe-NF electrode, which was in good agreement with the HERresults shown in Fig. 3 a. The solution resistances (R s) for the systems related to Pt/NF and Pt/Fe-NF electrode were 14.69 U and 9.893 U(Table 2 ), respectively, manifesting excellent conductivity of electrolyte. Various analyses and comparisons showed that Pt/Fe-NF electrode outperformed the counterparts of Pt/NF and NFelectrodes. This could be attributable to, on the one hand, the addition of Fe was beneﬁcial for Pt loading on the electrode; and on the other hand, and the synergetic effect resulted fromthe formation of Fe-Ni alloy during the preparation process.In our case, it may easily be assumed that the enhancedperformance of Pt/Fe-NF was due to the higher amount ofPt loading on the electrode. However, it was believed thatthe addition of Fe also played a signiﬁcant role. The reason for this could be the for- mation of Fe and Ni alloy, which leads to the change of elec-tronic structure and exhibits synergistic effect. Meanwhile,there may be another synergistic effect between Fe and Pt [27]. Conclusions The Pt/Fe-NF electrode was successfully fabricated using electrodeposition and impregnation deposition technique forHER. Various characterization results of the SEM-EDS, ICP-AES, XRD, XPS, LSV, CV, EIS and Tafel curves demonstratedthat the Pt/Fe-NF electrode exhibited higher electrocatalyticactivity than those of Pt/NF and NF electrodes. This was likelydue to, (a) the addition of Fe is beneﬁcial to the Pt loading onNF electrode; (b) the formation of the alloy of Fe and Ni, whichplayed the role of the synergetic effect for HER; (c) its enlargedelectrochemical area and rich active sites with high double-layer capacitance. The as-synthesized Pt/Fe-NF electrode with desirable electrochemical activity is a promising elec- trode material for industrial hydrogen production from alka-line electrolyte. Zengc,1,  Chen  Yeb,d,  Yangguang  Zhub,e,  Feiyue  Chenb,f, Mingyang  Yangb,d,  Li  Fug,  Shiyu  Duh,  Jinhong  Yub,d,  Nan  Jiangb,d,  Jianxiong  Liua,**, Tianzhun  Wuc,**,  Cheng-Te  Linb,d,* China fCollege  of  Science,  Henan  University  of  Technology,  Zhengzhou  10463,  China gCollege  of  Materials  and  Environmental  Engineering,  Hangzhou  Dianzi  University,  Hangzhou  310018,  China hNingbo  Institute  of  Materials  Technology  and  Engineering  (NIMTE),  Chinese  Academy  of  Sciences,  Ningbo  315201,  China A  R  T  I  C  L  E  I  N  F  O Article  history: Received  30  December  2019 Received  in  revised  form  28  February  2020 Accepted  11  March  2020 Available  online  12  March  2020 Keywords: Platinum  nanodendrites Hydrogen  evolution  reaction Electrocatalytic  activity Long-term  durability pH-universal  propertyA  B  S  T  R  A  C  T High-performance  nanomaterial  catalysts  for  hydrogen  evolution  reaction  via  electrochemical  water splitting  are  signiﬁcant  to  the  development  of  hydrogen  energy.  In  this  work,  we  report  a  robust  and highly  active  catalyst  fabricated  through  direct  electrochemical  deposition  of  Pt  nanodendrites  at  the surface  of  activated  carbon  (Pt  NDs).  Owing  to  the  large  electrochemically  active  area  and  the  exposed (111)  facet  of  Pt,  Pt  NDs  exhibits  outstanding  activity  towards  hydrogen  evolution  reaction  with  a  low requiring  overpotential  of  0.027  V  at  10  mA/cm2and  Tafel  slope  of  /C25  22  mV/dec  in  acidic  media.  In addition,  the  hydrogen  yield  of  Pt  NDs  is  30%–45%  larger  than  that  of  commercial  Pt/C  at  the  same  Pt loadings.  Moreover,  Pt  NDs  exhibits  excellent  long-term  durability  whose  hydrogen  production  efﬁciency remains  unchanged  after  six-hour  hydrogen  production,  while  the  efﬁciency  of  commercial  Pt/C  catalyst decayed  9%  under  the  same  circumstance.  Considering  the  superiority  of  catalytic  activity  and  stability, this  Pt  NDs  present  great  potentiality  towards  practical  hydrogen  production  application. ©  2020  Chinese  Chemical  Society  and  Institute  of  Materia  Medica,  Chinese  Academy  of  Medical  Sciences. Published  by  Elsevier  B.V.  All  rights  reserved. With  the  population  increasing  and  economic  developing,  large demand  of  energy  has  become  a  common  problem  for  human beings.  So  far,  non-renewable  fossil  fuel  occupies  85%  of  world energy  consumption,  which  contributes  to  numerous  environ- mental  issues,  including  global  warming,  acid  rain  and  ozone depletion,  etc.  [1,2].  Under  the  pressure  of  resources  and environment,  it  is  of  great  signiﬁcance  to  establish  a  new  energy system  based  on  renewable  resources  [3].  Among  renewableresources,  hydrogen  (H2)  is  regarded  as  a  promising  alternative  to fossil  fuel,  not  only  because  of  its  zero-carbon  emission  but  also  the high  gravimetric  energy  density  (120/C0142  MJ/kg)  [4,5].  Besides, energy  conversion  between  H2and  electricity  can  be  easily executed  through  water  electrolysis  and  hydrogen-oxygen  fuel  cell [6,7].  In  order  to  realize  the  widespread  use  of  H2,  efﬁcient  mass production  technologies  are  needed,  including  catalytic  steam reforming  and  coal  gasiﬁcation  as  the  representatives  [8,9].  As  the improvement  of  solar  cell  and  wind  power  installation,  using  H2to store  surplus  solar  and  wind  energy  becomes  more  promising. Therefore,  electrochemical  water  splitting  is  attracting  researchers as  a  fossil  fuel-free  strategy  to  produce  H2with  high  purity  [9].  To achieve  the  efﬁcient,  stable  and  scalable  electrochemical  H2 production,  developing  high  performance  catalysts  for  hydrogen evolution  reaction  (HER)  is  the  key  point. In  various  catalysts,  platinum  (Pt)  is  recognized  as  one  of  the most  effective  HER  catalyst,  which  has  nearly  zero  overpotential*  Corresponding  author  at:  Key  Laboratory  of  Marine  Materials  and  Related Technologies,  Zhejiang  Key  Laboratory  of  Marine  Materials  and  Protective Technologies,  Ningbo  Institute  of  Materials  Technology  and  Engineering  (NIMTE), Chinese  Academy  of  Sciences,  Ningbo  315201,  China. **  Corresponding  authors. content:

and  outstanding  long-term  durability  [10–12].  However,  as  a typical  noble  metal,  high  cost  of  Pt  makes  people  seek  the alternatives  [13].  Over  the  past  decade,  with  the  rise  of  nano- materials,  many  researchers  turned  their  eyes  on  novel  Pt-free  HER nanocatalysts  [14]  to  partly  replace  Pt.  On  one  side,  non-noble metals  nanomaterials  were  widely  investigated,  including  transi- tion  metal  carbides  [15–17],  chalcogenides  [18,19],  nitrides  [20] and  phosphides  [21]  as  well  as  metal  alloys  [22].  For  example,  Yao et  al.  synthesized  three-dimensional  Ni2P  nanoparticles  which exhibited  overpotential  of  69  mV  at  10  mV/cm2and  Tafel  slope  of 55  mV/dec  in  acidic  media  [23].  Yang  et  al.  prepared  CoP  nanosheet with  a  Tafel  slope  of  30.1  mV/dec,  which  is  close  to  the performance  of  Pt  [24].  On  another  side,  various  carbon-based nanomaterials  were  adopted  as  metal-free  catalyst  [25–27]. Although  these  catalysts  are  cost  saving  from  the  perspective  of preparation,  they  still  suffer  in  high  HER  overpotential  and  low hydrogen  production  efﬁcient,  which  leads  to  larger  power consumption.  Recently,  in  order  to  overcome  the  difﬁculty, researchers  manage  to  design  nanostructure  for  Pt  catalyst  to enhance  the  catalytic  property  without  increasing  the  yield  of  Pt [28,29].  For  instance,  Jiang  et  al.  synthesized  Pt  nanocrystals  which achieve  a  low  Tafel  slope  of  28  mV/dec  and  large  exchange  current density  of  0.99  mA/cm2[30].  Hou  et  al.  electro-deposited  Pt nanoparticles  as  HER  electrode,  whose  Tafel  slope  is  estimated  to be  53.6  mV/dec  [31].  These  studies  provide  inspiration  to  construct nanostructure  of  noble  metal  and  indicate  that  the  performance  of HER  catalyst  can  be  further  improved. In  this  work,  we  fabricated  nanoscale  Pt  dendrites  at  the  surface of  activated  carbon  (Pt  NDs)  by  electrodeposition.  Obtained  Pt nanodendrites  exposed  numerous  atoms  at  (111)  facet  which possess  better  catalytic  activity  than  amorphous  Pt  [32].  Electro- chemical  measurements  showed  that  the  Pt  NDs  catalyst  exhibits excellent  activity  with  a  low  Tafel  slope  (22.2  mV/dec),  while  that of  commercial  Pt/C  at  the  same  Pt  loading  is  28.6  mV/dec.Moreover,  Pt  NDs  also  displays  good  electrochemical  stability and  pH-universal  property.  Among  acidic,  neutral  and  alkaline environment,  Pt  NDs  shows  30%–45%  performance  enhancement in  actual  hydrogen  production  comparing  to  commercial  Pt/C catalyst. The  fabrication  process  for  the  preparation  of  Pt  NDs  as  the hydrogen  evolution  catalyst  was  illustrated  in  Fig.  1a.  Firstly, 200  mg  activated  carbon  (AC)  powder  was  dispersed  in  mixed solvent  of  5  mL  ethanol  and  5  mL  de-ionized  water  by  30-min ultrasonication.  Then  500  mL  5%  Naﬁon  solution  was  added  into dispersion  to  form  uniform  activated  carbon  ink.  2  mL  activated carbon  ink  was  drop-coated  and  naturally  dried  at  the  surface  of glassy  carbon  electrode  (GCE)  to  form  the  activated  carbon electrode.  Prior  to  electrodeposition,  Pt  chloride  solution (10  mmol/L  (NH 4)2PtCl 6,  7.5  mmol/L  PtCl 4,  25  mmol/L  NaH 2PO4, and  425  mmol/L  Na2HPO 4)  was  prepared  and  degassed  by  nitrogen bubbling.  A  three-electrode  system  was  composed  of  AC  electrode, Pt  plate  electrode  and  Hg/HgCl 2electrode  as  working,  counter  and reference  electrode,  respectively.  The  Pt  nanodendrites  were electrodeposited  onto  AC  electrode  in  Pt  chloride  solution  at  a constant  potential  of  /C00.6  V  vs.  Ag/AgCl  for  900  s  [33]. The  scanning  electron  microscopy  (SEM)  images  of  glassy carbon  electrode  (GCE),  AC  layer  and  Pt  NDs  are  displayed  in Figs.  1b–e,  which  present  the  change  of  morphology  on  the electrode  surface.  Comparing  to  smooth  surface  of  GCE  as  shown  in Fig.  1b,  AC  layer  is  a  sponge-like  structure  (Fig.  1c),  providing signiﬁcantly  larger  electrochemically  active  area  [34]  as  nucleation sites  for  Pt.  Therefore,  Pt  deposited  on  AC  layer  formed  nanoscale dendrite  structure  (Figs.  1d  and  e),  which  is  beneﬁcial  to  provide high  speci ﬁc  surface  area  with  large  amount  of  active  sites  and corner  atoms.  Through  energy  dispersive  spectroscopy  (EDS),  in- plane  elemental  mapping  of  C  and  Pt  of  Pt  NDs  were  obtained (Figs.  1f  and  g),  both  C  and  Pt  were  well  distributed,  suggesting  that our  electrodeposition  was  macroscopically  uniform. Fig.  1.  (a)  Scheme  of  the  preparation  process  of  Pt  NDs  catalyst  for  hydrogen  production.  (b–d)  SEM  top-view  images  of  glassy  carbon,  activated  carbon,  and  Pt  NDs.  (e) Regional  enlarged  view  of  nanodendrite-structure  of  (d).  (f,  g)  EDS  mapping  of  element  distribution  of  C  and  Pt.  (h)  TGA  curves  of  20%  Pt/C,  60%  Pt/C  and  Pt  NDs.H. content:
[接上页] Sun  et  al. /  Chinese  Chemical  Letters  31  (2020)  2478 –2482  2479

Thermogravimetric  analysis  (TGA)  was  also  executed  to  investigate the  deposition  mass  of  Pt.  As  shown  in  Fig.  1h,  after  complete degradation  of  AC,  the  mass  of  residual  Pt  is  57.6%  of  the  original mass.  Considering  the  Pt  mass  fraction,  we  applied  commercial 20%  Pt/C  and  60%  Pt/C  catalysts  (the  percentage  represents  the  Pt mass  fraction)  as  control  group,  whose  thermogravimetric  analysis (TGA)  curves  are  also  shown  in  Fig.  1h.  20%  Pt/C  is  the  most  widely used  commercial  catalyst,  and  60%  Pt/C  has  the  closest  Pt  mass fraction  to  Pt  NDs.  Note  that  the  curve  inﬂection  points  reﬂect  the temperature  range  of  carbon  degradation.  The  range  of  Pt  NDs  is from  364/C0663/C14C,  while  that  of  commercial  Pt/C  is  from 293/C0485/C14C,  suggesting  that  Pt  NDs  shows  better  thermal  stability. The  variation  of  thermal  stability  could  be  attributed  to  the difference  of  catalyst  structure:  Commercial  Pt/C  catalyst  is  Pt nanoparticles-loaded  carbon  structure  owing  to  its  hydrolysis preparation  process,  while  Pt  NDs  has  Pt-encapsulated  carbon structure  formed  by  electrodeposition  which  is  protective  for carbon  [35]. In  order  to  get  the  crystalline  information  of  nanodendrites,  Pt NDs  was  characterized  through  transmission  electron  microscopy (TEM).  In  the  TEM  bright  ﬁeld  image  of  Pt  NDs,  although  there  are some  amorphous  regions,  the  overall  crystalline  of  the  sample  is good  and  clear  lattice  fringes  can  be  observed  (Fig.  2a).  Through counting  the  lattice  fringes  in  the  image  with  atomic  resolution, the  spacing  of  lattice  plane  is  0.226  nm,  corresponding  to  the  (111) facet  of  Pt  (Fig.  2b).  The  result  of  selected-area  electron  diffraction (SAED)  measurement  shows  diffraction  patterns  of  (111),  (220), (311)  facet  of  Pt  (Fig.  2c).  In  addition,  Pt  NDs  was  also  investigated with  X-ray  diffraction  (XRD)  using  Cu-K α  radiation.  As  shown  in Fig.  2d,  ﬁve  sharp  diffraction  peaks  in  Pt  NDs  are  indexed  to  Pt(111),  Pt  (200),  Pt  (220),  Pt  (311)  and  Pt  (222),  respectively,  which match  well  with  face-centered  cubic  (FCC)  platinum  (PDF  No.  04- 0802)  [36,37].  Two  wide  peaks  are  attributed  to  crystalline  carbon in  AC  [38],  which  can  be  also  observed  in  XRD  curves  of  commercial Pt/C  catalysts.  However,  Pt  peaks  of  commercial  Pt/C  catalysts  are unconspicuous  compared  to  Pt  NDs.  These  results  proved  that  Pt atoms  deposited  on  Pt  NDs  are  in  good  crystallinity,  and  the preferred  lattice  plane  is  (111)  facet.  In  contrast,  Pt  in  commercial Pt/C  catalysts  is  more  amorphous,  because  of  their  preparation method  of  precursor  hydrolysis  (Fig.  2e). Pt  has  been  regarded  as  one  of  the  best  catalyst  for  hydrogen production,  due  to  the  Gibbs  free  energy  nearest  to  zero  toward HER  reaction  [32,39].  Moreover,  comparing  to  pure  Pt,  Pt  (111)  has larger  exchange  current  in  equilibrium,  which  has  been  already proved  theoretically  and  experimentally  [39–42].  Therefore,  we expect  that  Pt  NDs  with  numerous  Pt  atoms  at  (111)  facet  has higher  electrochemical  activity  than  commercial  Pt/C.  The electrochemical  performance  of  electrocatalysts  was  measured using  a  three-electrode  system  in  0.5  mol/L  H2SO4.  Firstly,  the estimation  of  electrochemical  active  surface  area  of  20%  Pt/C,  60% Pt/C  and  Pt  NDs  was  determined  via  the  double  layer  capacitance (Cdll)  in  cyclic  voltammetry  (CV)  curves.  Figs.  3a–c  show  the  CV curves  of  20%  Pt/C,  60%  Pt/C  and  Pt  NDs  at  a  series  of  scan  rates  (50, 100,  150,  200  and  250  mV/s).  The  Cdllof  these  catalysts  can  be determined  by  plotting  the  difference  of  current  density  at  0.4  V against  the  scan  rate,  which  is  generally  proportional  to  the  Cdll [43].  According  to  the  results,  the  Cdllof  20%  Pt/C,  60%  Pt/C  and  Pt NDs  is  3.3,  5.7  and  14.8  mF/cm2,  respectively  (Fig.  3d).  It demonstrates  that  Cdllof  Pt  NDs  at  the  similar  Pt  content  is  over two  times  larger  than  that  of  60%  Pt/C,  indicating  that Fig.  2. nanodendrite  structure  could  signiﬁcantly  enhance  electrochemi- cal  active  area.  Moreover,  the  linear  sweep  voltammetry  (LSV)  was performed  to  reveal  the  catalytic  activity.  As  shown  in  Fig.  3e,  the LSV  curves  shows  that  Pt  NDs  requires  ultrasmall  overpotential  of 27  mV  to  achieve  current  density  of  10  mA/cm2,  while  the  required overpotentials  of  20%  Pt/C  and  60%  Pt/C  are  44.6  and  36.3  mV, respectively.  A  smaller  overpotential  of  Pt  NDs  among  these catalysts  suggests  a  faster  increase  of  HER  rate  with  an  increase  of applied  potential.  In  order  to  highlight  the  merit  of  Pt  NDs,  catalytickinetics  was  evaluated  from  the  Tafel  plots  (Fig.  3f)  derived  from LSV  curves.  Pt  NDs  shows  a  Tafel  slope  of  22.2  mV/dec,  whereas 20%  Pt/C  and  60%  Pt/C  exhibit  Tafel  slopes  of  28.6  and  32.7  mV/dec, respectively.  The  Tafel  slope  reﬂects  the  rate-determining  step  in catalytic  process,  while  the  lowest  Tafel  slope  of  Pt  NDs  indicates that  Pt  NDs  is  more  active  toward  the  HER.  As  a  consequence,  Pt NDs  exhibit  excellent  catalytic  activity  exceeding  commercial  Pt/C catalysts,  which  relies  on  the  crystalline  Pt  and  nanodendrite structure  with  large  surface  area. In  order  to  study  the  actual  hydrogen  production  performance, catalysts  are  directly  used  in  aqueous  mediawith  different  pH  values under  applied  voltage  at  /C00.03  V.  The  quanti ﬁcation  of  hydrogen  gas was  executed  through  drainage  water  for  5  h  after  the  H2was saturated  in  electrolyte.  As  shown  in  Fig.  4a,  hydrogen  production rate  of  Pt  NDs  in  0.5  mol/L  H2SO4solution  (pH  /C25  1),  1/C2phosphate buffer  saline  (pH  /C25  7)  and  1.0  mol/L  KOH  solution  (pH  /C25  13)  is  0.52, 0.40  and  0.31  mL/h,  respectively.  The  practical  efﬁciency  of  Pt  NDs  is 30%–45%  higher  than  that  of  60%  Pt/C  catalysts  and  50%–75%  higher than  that  of  20%  Pt/C  catalysts,  suggesting  that  the  nanodendrite structure  design  shows  great  potential  for  HER  applications  with  pH- universal  property.  Besides,  Pt  NDs  shows  excellent  stability  in  long- term  galvanostatic  test  with  constant  current  density  of  150  mA/cm2 in  0.5  mol/L  H2SO4solution  (Fig.  4b).  During  the  six-hour  test,  the electrochemical  potential  of  Pt  NDs  remained  almost  constant. However,  the  performance  of  20%  Pt/C  and  60%  Pt/C  undergo  a  decay of  12%  and  9%  after  a  period  of  time.  It  should  be  noted  that  hydrogen bubbles  generated  from  commercial  Pt/C  is  larger  than  those  from  Pt NDs  [44],  which  can  cause  physical  shock  towards  catalyst  structure, leading  to  the  degradation  of  catalytic  performance.  The  electro- chemical  stability  of  Pt  NDs  was  also  investigated  by  continuous  CV scanning.  As  shown  in  Fig.  4c,  CV  curves  of  Pt  NDs  remain  nearly unchanged  even  after  5000  cycles.  When  after  1000  cycles,  3000 cycles  and  5000  cycles,  LSV  tests  were  also  executed  and  the  data  can be  seen  in  Fig.  4d.  These  LSV  curves  almost  overlap  with  each  other and  have  no  difference  with  initial  LSV  curves.  SEM  image  of  Pt  NDs after  above  tests  is  similar  to  initial  state  as  well,  suggesting  the sample  have  great  morphology  stability  (Fig.  4e).  As  a  consequence, these  ﬁndings  indicate  that  Pt  NDs  not  only  possesses  high  catalytic efﬁciency,  but  also  have  outstanding  stability  under  heavy  loading. In  summary,  we  developed  Pt  nanodendrites  at  activated  carbon catalyst  for  hydrogen  evolution  production,  which  was  prepared  by electrodepositing  Pt  onto  activated  carbon  template.  Pt  NDs  exhibits superior  catalytic  activity  with  a  low  overpotential  of  0.027  V  at 10  mA/cm2and  a  small  Tafel  slope  down  to  22.2  mV/dec.  This  could be  attributed  to  the  highly  active  Pt  atoms  at  preferred  (111)  facet  of the  electrodeposited  catalyst  and  large  surface  area  of  nanodendrite structure.  Besides,  Pt  NDs  could  produce  hydrogen  in  aqueous solution  with  different  pH,  whose  production  ratio  is  30%–45% higher  than  that  of  commercial  catalysts  at  the  same  Pt  loading. Moreover,  Pt  NDs  presents  prominent  stabilityeven  aftercontinuous reacting  for  6  h  or  5000  CV  cycles  in  0.5  mol/L  H2SO4.  This  work  could provideanalternativecatalystanditisexpectedtobeextendedtothe practical  hydrogen  production. file_name: Xing 等 - 2017 - Ultrafine Pt Nanoparticle-Decorated Co(OH)2 Nanosheet Arrays with Enhanced Catalytic Acti.pdf
content: Ultra ﬁne Pt Nanoparticle-Decorated Co(OH) 2Nanosheet Arrays with Enhanced Catalytic Activity toward Hydrogen Evolution Zhicai Xing,†Ce Han,†Dewen Wang,†,‡Qun Li,†,‡and Xiurong Yang *,† †State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, Jilin China ‡University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China *SSupporting Information ABSTRACT: The combinations of Earth-abundant materials with noble metals provide an orientation for developing highly active and stable catalysts towardhydrogen production with reduced noble metal loadings. Here, we designedcarbon cloth (CC)-supported Earth-abundant Co(OH) 2nanosheets array (Co(OH)2/CC) as an ideal three-dimensional (3D) substrate for Pt electro- deposition (Pt −Co(OH)2/CC, Pt in Pt −Co(OH)2: 5.7 wt %) to achieve top performance of a hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) under alkaline and neutralconditions. The Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst exhibits a near-zero onset over- potential and a Tafel slope of 70 mV dec−1, and it requires an overpotential of 32, 54, and 122 mV to deliver the geometrical current density of 10, 20, and 100 mA cm−2, respectively, with catalytic activities exceeding to those of the commercial Pt/C decorated CC (Pt/C/CC). Furthermore, the HER activity of Co(OH) 2decorated with several transition metals (Ni, Co, and Fe) was demonstrated in experiments, further validating the high HER activity of thePt−Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst. In addition, this catalyst also o ﬀers enhanced catalytic performance and durability under neutral conditions. Impressively, based on the normalized HER current densities by electrochemical surface area, the HER activity of thePt−Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst is 4.8 and 2.6 times greater than that of the commercial Pt/C/CC in alkaline and neutral solution, respectively. The unprecedented catalytic performances of Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst are attributed to the synergistic catalytic eﬀects originating from the nanointerfaces between Co(OH) 2and Pt. KEYWORDS: Co(OH) 2, Pt nanoparticles, reduced Pt loading, electrocatalyst, hydrogen evolution reaction With increasing energy costs and concerning global warming and climate change, hydrogen as a clean and renewable energy resource has gained grand attention for tackling the current energy crisis and environmental issues.1,2 Alkaline water electrolysis, because of its suitable manufacture safety, stable output, and high product purity, is considered asone of the most sustainable approaches for producinghydrogen. 3−5However, because of the inhibition of sluggish kinetics of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in alkalinemedia, the electrocatalysts always exhibit high overpotential and large energy consumption, further restricting their practical application in alkaline electrolysis. 6,7Platinum (Pt) as the state- of-art electrocatalyst for alkaline HER, but its activity is 2 ordersof magnitude lower than that for acidic HER. 6−8In addition, despite the great success that has been achieved based on Earth-abundant electrocatalysts that include transition-metal nitrides, sulﬁdes, and phosphides for alkaline HER, 2Pt-based electro- catalysts are still the preferred HER catalysts with unparalleledactivity. Therefore, new concepts that facilitate the develop-ment of catalysts with several unique features, including high Ptutilization, enhanced activity, and improved stability are stillchallenging. The combinations of the individual advantages of Pt and Earth-abundant materials have emerged as an e ﬀective strategy to enhance the HER activity of Pt in an alkaline environ-ment. 7,9−14Recently, the Markovic group electrochemically deposited Ni(OH) 2nanoclusters on a Pt electrode surface for catalyzing HER.7In their bifunctional materials, the edges of Ni(OH)2clusters promote the process of water dissociation and the generation of hydrogen intermediates that are subsequently adsorbed on the nearby Pt surface and ﬁnally recombines to form molecular hydrogen.7Following this strategy, Chen and co-workers further modi ﬁed a Pt3Ni nanoframe via the electrodeposition of Ni(OH) 2clusters for increasing the HER activity in alkaline electrolytes.9Based on these two works, Yin and co-workers synthesized ultrathin Pt nanowires (NWs) on single-layer (SL) Ni(OH)2nanosheets hybrid material (Pt content in the hybrid is 38.0 wt %) for constructing high-performance HER catalysts in basic sol- ution.10Recently, Zeng and co-workers further demonstrated that Ni (hydroxy)oxide/Pt(111) ﬁlm had enhanced HER activity through a bifunctional mechanism, using a combination of density functional theory and in situ characterization.13 However, all these catalysts involved the massive consumptionof the noble metal platinum (Pt as substrates;7,9,11−13Pt in Pt NWs/SL-Ni(OH) 2is 38 wt %;10Pt in Pt@2D-Ni(OH) 2is 43 Received: June 18, 2017 Revised: August 25, 2017 Published: September 21, 2017Letter pubs.acs.org/acscatalysis © 2017 American Chemical Society 7131 DOI: 10.1021/acscatal.7b01994 ACS Catal. 2017, 7, 7131 −7135 Downloaded via BEIJING UNIV OF CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY on March 20, 2026 at 09:20:55 (UTC). See https://pubs.acs.org/sharingguidelines for options on how to legitimately share published articles.

wt %14), which impedes its commercialization, thus o ﬀering us a new strategy for the development of novel Pt-based catalystswith distinguished activity and durability, while reducing theoverall Pt loading. Moreover, nanoarray catalysts have theirintrinsic advantages, including good electrical conductivity andhigh structural stability, large surface area for e ﬃcient utilization of active species, and reduced catalyst loading with enhancedelectrochemical performance. 15−19Therefore, in this work, we report on the development of Pt −Co(OH)2nanosheet arrays supported on carbon cloth (CC) (Pt −Co(OH)2/CC, Pt in Pt − Co(OH)2is 5.7 wt %) for exploiting the synergistic e ﬀects of Co(OH)2and Pt for the electrochemical HER. The Co(OH)2/ CC nanosheets array with low-cost and high surface area wasnot only employed as an ideal 3D substrate for Ptelectrodeposition with high utilization rate of Pt, but also served as a new form of synergistic catalyst for tremendously enhanced hydrogen generation. As a proof-of-concept, theobtained Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst exhibits unprecedented catalytic activity and durability toward HER under basic andneutral conditions. The Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC electrocatalyst was fabricated via the hydrothermal synthesis of Co(OH) 2/CC, followed by the electrodeposition of Pt, as illustrated in Figure 1 . CC was applied as a three-dimensional (3D) support for integrating HER catalysts; a representative scanning electron microscopy(SEM) image of CC is shown in Figure S1a in the Supporting Information. Co(OH) 2nanosheets were densely grown on the surface of CC to form Co(OH) 2/CC nanosheet arrays (see Figures S1b −S1din the Supporting Information), and such 3D Co(OH) 2/CC substrates exhibit high porosity with large Brunauer −Emmett −Teller (BET) surface areas (up to 71.6 m2g−1) (see Figure S2 in the Supporting Information). Then, Pt was electrodeposited on the surface of the Co(OH)2/CC to form Pt −Co(OH)2/CC nanosheet arrays. The X-ray di ﬀraction (XRD) pattern of Pt −Co(OH)2/CC shows that no Pt- containing crystal phases can be observed (see Figure 2 a),20 because the Pt content in Pt −Co(OH)2nanosheets array is ∼5.7 wt % (<2 wt % when taking the CC into account), which is quantitatively measured with an inductively coupled plasmamass spectrometry (ICP-MS) technique. According to the SEM images (see Figures 2 b and 2c) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image ( Figure 2 d), no large Pt particles were observed, which was consistent with scanning tunnellingelectron microscopy (STEM) images and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping images showing the homogeneousdistribution of Co, O, and Pt over the nanosheet ( Figure S3 in the Supporting Information). After electrodeposition of Pt,smaller nanoparticles are observed on a Co(OH) 2nanosheet with diameters ranging from 2.0 nm to 3.5 nm (see Figure 2 e). The high-resolution TEM image shows that the lattice spacingof∼0.224 nm agreed well with the (111) plane of Pt (see Figure 2 f).21−23Figure S4 in the Supporting Information shows the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum in the Pt4f region for Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC. The binding energies of Pf 4f7/2and Pt 4f 5/2of Pt−Co(OH) 2were 71.0 and 74.4 eV, respectively, which are similar to the binding energies ofpreviously reported Pt-based catalysts,21,23suggesting that all the Pt nanoparticles coated on the Co(OH) 2nanosheet arrays are mainly metallic Pt. The above results demonstrate thatsmaller Pt nanoparticles were successfully decorated on thesurface of Co(OH) 2/CC nanosheet arrays via electrodeposition techniques. The electrocatalytic activities of the Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC (Pt − Co(OH) 2loading: 6.9 mg cm−2) toward HER normalized by geometric surface area (GSA, 1 cm ×1 cm) along with Pt- decorated CC (Pt/CC), Co(OH)2/CC, blank CC, and commercial Pt/C (20 wt % Pt/XC-72) loaded CC (Pt/C/CC) catalysts were performed in N 2-saturated 1.0 M KOH solution to verify the synergistic e ﬀects (see Figure 3 ). The Nyquist plots of Pt −Co(OH)2/CC and Co(OH)2/CC shown inFigure 3 a, as well as Figure S5 in the Supporting Information indicate that the ionic and ohmic resistances of Pt −Co(OH)2/ CC are much smaller than those of Co(OH)2/CC, implying that the electrodeposition of Pt can obviously enhance the electronic conductivity of Co(OH) 2/CC. In the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves on the reversible hydrogenelectrode (RHE) scale, a near-zero onset overpotential forthe Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC was observed, which was ∼210 mV lower than that of Co(OH) 2/CC ( Figure 3 b). hydrogen evolution. The Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst ap- proaches current densities of 10, 20, and 100 mA cm−2at overpotentials of 32, 54, and 122 mV, respectively, and thesevalues are smaller than those for Pt/C/CC (38, 56, and 123mV) (see Figure 3 b, as well as Figure S6 in the Supporting Information). In contrast, Pt/CC exhibits inferior HER activity,because overpotentials of 75, 104, and 185 mV are needed todeliver the same current densities, although its onset over-potential is similar to those of Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC and Pt/C/CC (see Figure S6 ). The undoubtedly remarkable HER perform- ances of Pt −Co(OH)2/CC electrocatalyst were the best among all reported values for transition-metal- and noble-metal-basedHER catalysts in basic media with current densities normalizedto GSA of electrodes (see Table S1 in the Supporting Information). The LSV curves of Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC under di ﬀerent Pt electrodeposition times are shown in Figure S7 in the Supporting Information, and its catalytic activity ﬁrst increased and then decreased as the deposition time increased from 5 min to 20 min, which is consistent with the previous report.24When the electrodeposition time was 15 min, the Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst exhibits the highest electrocatalytic activity. Tounderstand the original di ﬀerences in HER performance of Pt/CC, Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC, and Pt/C/CC, we measured the capacitances of the double-layer at the solid/liquid interface ofthese electrodes by collecting cyclic voltammograms (CVs) in1.0 M KOH ( Figure S8 in the Supporting Information) to estimate their electrochemical surface area (ECSA).18,25Figure 3c shows the LSV curves of these three catalysts with current densities normalized to ECSA. The HER current density of Pt − Co(OH) 2/CC at an overpotential of 0.07 V is 1.518 mA cm−2, which is much higher than that of Pt/CC (0.361 mA cm−2) and commercial Pt/C/CC (0.318 mA cm−2), respectively. The performance of the Pt −Co(OH)2/CC catalyst is comparable to that observed for the currently reported electrocatalysts (thecurrent densities normalized to ECSA) in alkaline media (seeTable S2 in the Supporting Information). Note that the current density of Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC is 4.2 and 4.8 times higher than that of Pt/CC and commercial Pt/C/CC counterpart,respectively. Figure 3 d shows the corresponding Tafel plots for further analyzing their HER kinetics. The value of Pt − Co(OH) 2/CC (70 mV dec−1) is smaller than that of Pt/CC (93 mV dec−1) and Co(OH)2(125 mV dec−1); however, it is slightly larger than that of commercial Pt/C/CC (68 mVdec −1). In alkaline media, the Volmer step (H 2O+e−→Had+ OH−) is the rate-limiting step for Pt/C as HER catalysts.26,27 Therefore, for the Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst, the Co(OH) 2 nanosheet array provides active sites for water dissociation and therefore accelerates the Volmer step under basic conditions,leading to a markedly improved HER activity. The stability of the Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC electrocatalyst during repeated cycling in alkaline electrolyte was further evaluated.The Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst exhibits no obvious loss of activity after 1000 CV sweeps between +50 mV to −250 mV vs RHE at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. The overpotential changed to 136 mV to produce a current density of 100 mA cm−2after 1000 cycles, whereas the corresponding overpotential initially required is 122 mV, suggesting its high stability under alkalineconditions (see Figure 3 e). The morphology and structure of Pt−Co(OH) 2nanosheet arrays can be well-retained after cycling for 1000 cycles (see Figure S9 in the Supporting Information). Figure S10 in the Supporting Information shows the XPS spectrum of the Pt 4f region for the Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC electrode after 1000 cycles. The positions for the Pt 4f 7/2and Pt 4f5/2peaks of metallic Pt exhibit almost no shift after cycling. Combined with the Pt content in Pt −Co(OH) 2nanosheet arrays (5.5 wt %) after cycling, which measured by ICP-MS technique, these results suggest a strong interaction betweenthe Pt and Co(OH) 2/CC substrate, indicating good stability of Pt−Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst. Chronopotentiometric ( v−t) re- sponses of Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC and Pt/C/CC catalysts at a constant cathodic current density of 10 mA cm−2were tested for 20 h ( Figure 3 f). For the benchmark Pt/C/CC catalyst, the potential required to deliver a current density of 10 mA cm−2 decreases rapidly in the chronopotentiometric curve, in agreement with our previous report.28Impressively, under the same conditions, the corresponding potential for Pt −Co- (OH)2/CC catalyst shows considerably slower decay than that for commercial Pt/C/CC.10Theses results con ﬁrm that Co(OH) 2nanosheet arrays could e ﬀectively improve the long-term stability of Pt in basic media, being attributed tothe superior durability of Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC. Considering the anomalous enhancement of catalytic activity over Co(OH) 2/CC via Pt electrodeposition, the HER activity of Co(OH) 2/CC decorated with other transition metals (Ni, Co, and Fe) were also investigated. Therefore, Ni-decoratedCo(OH) 2/CC (Ni −Co(OH) 2/CC, Ni in Ni −Co(OH) 2: 5.6 wt %, by ICP-MS), Fe −Co(OH) 2/CC (Fe in Fe −Co(OH) 2: 5.8 wt %, by ICP-MS), and Co −Co(OH) 2/CC catalysts were Figure 3. freshly fabricated following the similar preparation procedure as Pt−Co(OH) 2/CC. As shown in Figure 4 , all these three catalysts exhibit higher HER activity than that of Co(OH) 2/CC (Figure 3 b). Among them, Ni −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst gives a current density of 10 mA cm−2at an overpotential of 163 mV, thereby largely reducing the corresponding overpotential of∼125 mV, compared with Co(OH) 2/CC. Consequently, under our experimental conditions, the trend of HER activity of thesedecorated catalysts is Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC > Ni −Co(OH) 2/CC >F e−Co(OH) 2/CC > Co −Co(OH) 2/CC, which is in good agreement with their results of electrical impedance spectra(see Figure 3 a, as well as Figure S11 in the Supporting Information), further corroborate that the Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst possesses the best catalytic activity and durabilitytoward HER. In addition to functioning as a highly e ﬃcient HER electrocatalyst in alkaline media, the Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst also served as highly active HER electrocatalysts under neutralconditions (1.0 M phosphate bu ﬀer solution, PBS). For comparison, the commercial Pt/C/CC benchmark catalystexhibits a high HER activity with an overpotential of 42 mV at acurrent density of 10 mA cm −2(see Figure 5 a). As expected, the Pt−Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst produces the current densities of 10 and 100 mA cm−2at overpotentials of 84 and 253 mV, respectively, which is much lower than those for Co(OH) 2/CC (328 mV) and Pt/CC (122 mV) at the current density of 10mA cm −2(Figure 5 a). These overpotentials are the lowest among the reported high-performance HER catalysts in neutralmedia (the current densities normalized to GSA), except forthat of the commercial Pt/C/CC (see Table S1 ). As shown in Figure 5 b, at an overpotential of 0.07 V, the current densities normalized to ECSA for the commercial Pt/C/CC, Pt/CC, andPt−Co(OH) 2/CC are 0.137, 0.188, and 0.335 mA cm−2, respectively. Note that the HER activity of Pt −Co(OH)2/CC is 2.6 times higher than that of commercial Pt/C/CC. The Pt − Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst also possesses excellent HER stability; its catalytic activity did not change after 1000 CV sweeps (seeFigure 5 c). In summary, we have developed a Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC electrocatalyst, whereby ultra ﬁne Pt nanoparticles were intimately interfaced with Earth-abundant Co(OH) 2nano- sheets with a low noble-metal content of 5.7 wt %, for highlyeﬃcient hydrogen evolution. Our experimental results demon- strate that the Pt −Co(OH) 2/CC catalyst exhibits an almost- zero onset overpotential and a top-level electrocatalytic activityfor HER, which is 4.8 and 2.6 times higher than that of thecommercial Pt/C in alkaline and neutral electrolyzes,respectively. More importantly, regarding the similarity of thequalitative trends for Co (hydroxy)oxide and Ni (hydroxy)-oxide ﬁlms on Pt (111), 13,14the unprecedented catalytic performances of the Pt-CO(OH) 2/CC electrocatalyst are attributed primarily to the synergistic e ﬀect of the Co(OH) 2 nanosheet array and high utilized Pt.13,14,28−30To our knowledge, this work provides the ﬁrst systematic study of how metal hydroxide nanosheet arrays can be applied as ideal3D substrates for growing noble metal to enhance theirelectrochemical HER activity, and, therefore, it o ﬀers a new strategy for designing e ﬃcient and multifunctional electro- catalysts for HER, oxygen evolution and reduction reactions,and beyond. ■ASSOCIATED CONTENT *SSupporting Information The Supporting Information is available free of charge on theACS Publications website at DOI: 10.1021/acscatal.7b01994 . content: Platinum Single Atoms Supported on Nanoarray-Structured Nitrogen-Doped Graphite Foil with Enhanced Catalytic Performance for Hydrogen Evolution Reaction Jingsong Xu,§Rui Li,§Rongguang Zeng, Xiayan Yan, Qingkai Zhao, Jingwen Ba, Wenhua Luo, * and Daqiao Meng * Cite This: ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2020, 12, 38106 −38112 Read Online ACCESS Metrics & More Article Recommendations *sıSupporting Information ABSTRACT: Platinum-based single-atom catalysts (SACs) are among the most promising candidates for the practical applications of electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), but their catalytic e ﬃciency remains to be further enhanced. Herein, a well- designed nanoarray-structured nitrogen-doped graphite foil(NNGF) substrate is introduced to support Pt SACs in Pt −N 4 construction (Pt 1/NNGF) for HER. Within NNGF, the constructed nanoarray-structured sur ﬁcial layer for supporting Pt SACs could enhance the exposure of active sites to the electrolyteand improve the reaction and di ﬀusion kinetics; meanwhile, the retained graphite structures in bulk NNGF provide not only therequired electrical conductivity but also the mechanical stabilityandﬂexibility. Because of such double-layer structures of NNGF, stable Pt −N 4construction, and binder-free advantages, the Pt 1/ NNGF electrode exhibits a low overpotential of 0.023 V at 10 mA cm−2and a small Tafel slope of 29.1 mV dec−1as well as an excellent long-term durability. KEYWORDS: nitrogen-doped graphite foil, plasma treatment, nanoarray structure, photochemical reduction, Pt single atoms, hydrogen evolution reaction ■INTRODUCTION With the increasing energy demand and worldwide environ- mental concerns, the exploration of reliable and renewable sources of clean energy becomes more and more urgent.1,2The generation of hydrogen (H 2) fuel through water electrolysis from renewable electricity is believed to be the most promising pathway toward the sustainable economy.3,4To realize the practical application of electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), an economical, e ﬃcient, and durable catalyst is required to accelerate the catalytic kinetics and reduce the required overpotential.5,6Among the developed electro- catalysts, Pt-based catalysts are the most active ones for HER catalysis because of the optimal binding energy of the Pt −H bond.7,8Considering the rocketing price and scarcity of Pt, it is highly desirable to maintain or enhance its HER e ﬃciency with as few dosages as possible. Currently, several strategies have been proposed to reduce the Pt loading amount in catalysts, including downsizing Pt nanoparticles, fabrication of Pt-based alloy and combination of Pt with nonprecious metal-based catalysts, and so forth.9−11Among them, carbonaceous materials-supported Pt single-atom catalysts (SACs) have attracted substantial resear ch attention because of their maximum atom utilization e ﬃciency and great potential toachieve high activities toward HER.12−21For example, single- atom Pt on graphdiyne prepared via the thermal processexhibits mass activity up to 26.9 times higher than that ofcommercial Pt/C catalysts. 22 Notably, for high-e ﬃciency HER process, it generally requires the electrode to be able to deal with fast electronand mass transportation, prompt and e ﬃcient gas release, and long-term durability. 9A general strategy is to construct a hybrid three dimensional (3D) electrocatalysts with hierarch-ical nanostructures on the sur ﬁcial layer for catalysis such as dendritic structure, nanowire array, and nanosheet array. 23,24 These nanostructures could be derived from either active species or supports, which would generate several concomitantbeneﬁts, including reduced interaction between bubbles and electrodes, increased electrochemical active surface area (ECSA), and accelerated reaction and di ﬀusion kinetics. 25,26 Received: May 26, 2020 Accepted: July 30, 2020 Published: July 30, 2020 Research Article www.acsami.org © 2020 American Chemical Society 38106https://dx.doi.org/10.1021/acsami.0c09615 ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2020, 12, 38106 −38112 Downloaded via BEIJING UNIV OF CHEMICAL TECHNOLOGY on March 20, 2026 at 09:23:07 (UTC).
